questions.q: Given a cover $\mathcal{U}=\{U_\alpha\}$ of $X$ and a sheaf $\mathcal{F}$, a $1$-cocycle is a family $\{t_{\alpha\beta}\in\mathcal{F}(U_{\alpha\beta})\}$ satisfying which identity on $U_{\alpha\beta\gamma}$?
questions.explain: The alternating Čech differential gives $(d t)_{\alpha\beta\gamma}=t_{\beta\gamma}-t_{\alpha\gamma}+t_{\alpha\beta}$; setting it to zero is the cocycle condition on triple overlaps.

choices: Because $\mathcal{F}$ is a constant sheaf on $X$.
Because a $0$-cocycle is a family of local sections agreeing on overlaps, which is a global section of $\mathcal{F}$.
questions.explain: A $0$-cochain is $\{s_\alpha\in\mathcal{F}(U_\alpha)\}$; $(ds)_{\alpha\beta}=s_\beta-s_\alpha=0$ forces the $s_\alpha$ to glue by the sheaf axiom, yielding exactly a global section.

questions.q: For the circle $S^1$ with the constant sheaf $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}$ and a standard good cover by two overlapping arcs whose intersection has two connected components, compute $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}} (\check{H}^1(S^1,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})\otimes\mathbb{Q})$.
questions.explain: $\check{H}^1(S^1,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})\cong\mathbb{Z}$ (the winding-number class), so rationally it is $1$-dimensional.

hard.q: For the torus $T^2$ with the constant sheaf $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}$, which statement about a cover by two open sets is correct?
choices: Any cover by two contractible sets is Leray, so $\check{H}^1=0$
No cover by two sets can compute $\check{H}^1(T^2,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})\cong\mathbb{Z}^2$ correctly because two-set Čech complexes truncate at $\check{C}^1$
A cover by two contractible sets is NOT Leray because their intersection need not be acyclic, and the Čech complex may miss $H^2$
The Čech complex with two sets always computes all cohomology of $T^2$
hard.explain: Two contractible open sets $U,V$ covering $T^2$ must have $U\cap V\simeq S^1\sqcup S^1$ (not contractible), so the cover fails the Leray acyclicity condition on intersections. This is precisely why a good cover (all intersections contractible) is required for Čech to recover sheaf cohomology.

hard.q: For the cover of $S^2$ by two contractible open hemispheres $U,V$ with $U\cap V\simeq S^1$, compute $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}}\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U},\underline{\mathbb{Q}})$ directly from the Čech complex $\check{C}^0\to\check{C}^1$.
hard.explain: $\check{C}^0=\underline{\mathbb{Q}}(U)\oplus\underline{\mathbb{Q}}(V)\cong\mathbb{Q}^2$ and $\check{C}^1=\underline{\mathbb{Q}}(U\cap V)$. Since $U\cap V\simeq S^1$, $H^0(U\cap V,\underline{\mathbb{Q}})\cong\mathbb{Q}$. The coboundary $d\colon\mathbb{Q}^2\to\mathbb{Q}$, $(a,b)\mapsto b-a$, is surjective with kernel $\mathbb{Q}$, giving $\ker/\mathrm{im}=0$. So $\check{H}^1=0$, consistent with $H^1(S^2)=0$.

hard.q: Which is the correct order of steps to verify that a Čech cocycle $\{t_{\alpha\beta}\}$ is a coboundary?
choices: Find $\{s_\alpha\in\mathcal{F}(U_\alpha)\}$ such that $t_{\alpha\beta}=s_\beta - s_\alpha$ on $U_{\alpha\beta}$, then check the cocycle condition
Check the cocycle condition $t_{\beta\gamma}-t_{\alpha\gamma}+t_{\alpha\beta}=0$, then declare it a coboundary
Find $\{s_\alpha\}$ with $s_\beta - s_\alpha = t_{\alpha\beta}$; if such $s_\alpha$ exist, the cocycle is a coboundary
Compute $H^1$ and if it vanishes declare every cocycle a coboundary without finding the $s_\alpha$
hard.explain: A $1$-cochain $\{t_{\alpha\beta}\}$ is a coboundary iff there exist local sections $s_\alpha\in\mathcal{F}(U_\alpha)$ with $t_{\alpha\beta}=s_\beta|_{U_{\alpha\beta}}-s_\alpha|_{U_{\alpha\beta}}$. Exhibiting explicit $s_\alpha$ is the direct verification; the cocycle condition is necessary but not sufficient.

questions.q: A cover $\mathcal{U} = \{U_\alpha\}$ of $X$ is Leray for a sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ when:
choices: $H^i(U_{\alpha_0\cdots\alpha_p},\mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $i > 0$ and every finite intersection
questions.explain: The Leray condition asks that $\mathcal{F}$ be acyclic on every finite intersection of the cover. When satisfied, the canonical map $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U},\mathcal{F}) \to H^p(X,\mathcal{F})$ is an isomorphism for every $p$.

questions.q: Which of the following guarantees that an affine open cover of a scheme is Leray for any quasi-coherent sheaf?
choices: Serre's theorem: $H^i(\mathrm{Spec}\,R, \widetilde M) = 0$ for $i > 0$ and every $R$-module $M$
questions.explain: Serre proved that affine schemes have no higher quasi-coherent cohomology, so every finite intersection of affine opens (also affine when the scheme is separated) is $\mathcal{F}$-acyclic. Hence an affine open cover is Leray.

questions.q: The standard affine cover of $\mathbb{P}^2$ has three charts $U_0,U_1,U_2$. How many terms does the resulting Čech complex $\check C^0 \to \check C^1 \to \check C^2$ have in $\check C^1$?
questions.explain: $\check C^1$ indexes strictly-increasing pairs $(\alpha_0,\alpha_1)$ from $\{0,1,2\}$, so its rank is $\binom{3}{2} = 3$. (Triple intersections contribute $\binom{3}{3}=1$ term in $\check C^2$.)

hard.q: Let $X=\mathbb{P}^1_k$ with affine cover $U_0=\{x_0\ne 0\}$, $U_1=\{x_1\ne 0\}$, and $\mathcal{F}=\mathcal{O}(-3)$. Which statement correctly explains why this cover is Leray for $\mathcal{O}(-3)$?
choices: Both $U_0$ and $U_1$ are isomorphic to $\mathbb{A}^1$, so they are affine; $U_0\cap U_1\cong\mathbb{A}^1\setminus\{0\}$ is also affine. Serre's theorem gives $H^i(\mathrm{affine},\widetilde{M})=0$ for $i>0$.
The sheaf $\mathcal{O}(-3)$ has $H^0=0$, so the cover is trivially Leray.
The cover is Leray because $\mathbb{P}^1$ is a smooth curve.
Affine covers are Leray only for line bundles of non-negative degree.
hard.explain: Each chart and their intersection are affine schemes. Serre's vanishing theorem for quasi-coherent sheaves on affine schemes ensures $H^i>0$ vanishes on each piece, making the cover Leray and the Čech computation exact.

hard.q: Using the Leray cover $\{U_0,U_1\}$ of $\mathbb{P}^2$ (standard affine cover by three charts), the Čech complex for a quasi-coherent sheaf has terms in degrees $0,1,2$. How many terms does $\check{C}^2$ have?
hard.explain: $\check{C}^2$ indexes strictly-increasing triples from $\{0,1,2\}$: only $(0,1,2)$, giving $\binom{3}{3}=1$ term.

hard.q: If $\mathcal{U}$ is a Leray cover for $\mathcal{F}$ but NOT for $\mathcal{G}$, and there is a map $\mathcal{F}\to\mathcal{G}$, which conclusion is valid?
choices: Both Čech groups equal the derived-functor groups because any cover suffices
Neither Čech computation is valid once a non-Leray sheaf is involved
$H^p(X,\mathcal{F})=0$ for all $p$ whenever the cover is Leray for $\mathcal{F}$
hard.explain: The Leray isomorphism $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U},\mathcal{F})\cong H^p(X,\mathcal{F})$ applies sheaf-by-sheaf; $\mathcal{F}$ satisfies the hypothesis but $\mathcal{G}$ does not, so the Čech-to-derived comparison holds for $\mathcal{F}$ and may fail for $\mathcal{G}$.

questions.q: A short exact sequence of sheaves $0\to\mathcal{F}'\to\mathcal{F}\to\mathcal{F}''\to 0$ produces a long exact sequence whose connecting homomorphism $\delta$ goes in which direction?
choices: $\delta$ goes between $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{F}''$ at the same degree
questions.explain: The connecting map raises degree by one and jumps from the quotient sheaf back to the subsheaf: $\delta\colon H^p(\mathcal{F}'') \to H^{p+1}(\mathcal{F}')$. In Čech language it lifts a local section of $\mathcal{F}''$ to one of $\mathcal{F}$; the failure of the lifts to glue is a class in $H^{p+1}(\mathcal{F}')$.

questions.q: In the exponential sequence $0 \to \underline{\mathbb{Z}} \to \mathcal{O} \to \mathcal{O}^\times \to 0$ on $\mathbb{C}^\times$, what does $\delta\colon H^0(\mathcal{O}^\times) \to H^1(\underline{\mathbb{Z}})$ send the global section $z$ to?
choices: the winding number class, which generates $H^1(\mathbb{C}^\times,\underline{\mathbb{Z}}) = \mathbb{Z}$
questions.explain: On $\mathbb{C}^\times$ the global section $z$ has no single-valued logarithm, so its image under $\delta$ is non-trivial. Concretely the class is $1\in H^1(\mathbb{C}^\times,\underline{\mathbb{Z}}) = \mathbb{Z}$, the winding-number generator.

questions.q: From $0\to\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^1}(-1)\to\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^1}\to\mathcal{O}_p\to 0$ (skyscraper at a point) and the vanishing $H^i(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^1}(-1))=0$ for all $i$, deduce $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}_p)$.
questions.explain: The sandwiching $H^i(\mathcal{O}(-1)) = 0$ forces $H^0(\mathcal{O}) \xrightarrow{\sim} H^0(\mathcal{O}_p)$. Since $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}) = 1$, we get $\dim H^0(\mathcal{O}_p) = 1$ — the stalk at $p$.

hard.q: From the exponential sequence $0\to\underline{\mathbb{Z}}\to\mathcal{O}_X\to\mathcal{O}_X^\times\to 0$ on a compact Riemann surface $X$, the connecting map $\delta\colon H^1(X,\mathcal{O}_X^\times)\to H^2(X,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})$ sends a line bundle $L$ to which invariant?
hard.explain: The connecting homomorphism $\delta$ in the long exact sequence of the exponential sequence sends a holomorphic line bundle (class in $H^1(\mathcal{O}^\times)=\mathrm{Pic}(X)$) to its first Chern class in $H^2(X,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})\cong\mathbb{Z}$, which equals the degree of the line bundle.

hard.q: On $\mathbb{P}^1$, apply the long exact sequence of $0\to\mathcal{O}(-1)\to\mathcal{O}^{\oplus 2}\to\mathcal{O}(1)\to 0$ (Euler sequence twisted). Given $\dim H^0(\mathcal{O})=1$, $\dim H^0(\mathcal{O}(-1))=0$, $\dim H^1(\mathcal{O}(-1))=0$, compute $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(1))$.
hard.explain: From the long exact sequence: $0\to H^0(\mathcal{O}(-1))\to H^0(\mathcal{O})^2\to H^0(\mathcal{O}(1))\to H^1(\mathcal{O}(-1))\to\cdots$. Since the flanking terms vanish, the middle map is an isomorphism followed by a surjection: $\dim H^0(\mathcal{O}(1))=2\cdot 1=2$.

hard.q: Which is the correct order of steps to construct the connecting homomorphism $\delta\colon H^p(X,\mathcal{F}'')\to H^{p+1}(X,\mathcal{F}')$ from a SES $0\to\mathcal{F}'\to\mathcal{F}\to\mathcal{F}''\to 0$?
choices: Apply $\Gamma$ first, use surjectivity of $\mathcal{F}\to\mathcal{F}''$ at the level of injective resolutions, then form the snake map

Take injective resolutions $0\to I'^\bullet\to I^\bullet\to I''^\bullet\to 0$, apply $\Gamma$ to get a SES of complexes (using injectivity to split), then the snake lemma on cohomology yields $\delta$
Directly apply $\Gamma$ to the SES and read off $\delta$ from the failure of surjectivity on global sections
Use the five lemma to construct $\delta$ from the long exact homotopy sequence
hard.explain: The standard construction: embed each sheaf in an injective sheaf, extend to a SES of injective resolutions (which splits in each degree by injectivity of $I'^p$), apply $\Gamma$, and apply the snake lemma to the resulting SES of cochain complexes. The resulting boundary map on cohomology is $\delta$.

questions.q: Which property of the global-sections functor $\Gamma(X,-)$ is measured by the derived functors $R^i\Gamma$?
questions.explain: $\Gamma(X,-)$ is left exact but not right exact; $R^i\Gamma$ measures exactly the right-exactness defect via a long exact sequence.

questions.q: Which class of sheaves is NOT automatically $\Gamma$-acyclic?
choices: soft sheaves on a paracompact Hausdorff space
questions.explain: Injective, flabby, and soft (on paracompact Hausdorff) sheaves are all $\Gamma$-acyclic. A locally constant sheaf like $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}$ on $S^1$ has nonzero $H^1$, so it is not acyclic in general.

questions.q: On $\mathbb{P}^1_k$ the line bundle $\mathcal{O}(-5)$ has $h^0=0$. Using $\dim_k H^1(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(n))=\max(-n-1,0)$, compute $\dim_k H^1(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(-5))$.

hard.q: Which of these sheaves on $S^1$ is NOT $\Gamma$-acyclic, and why?
choices: Any flabby sheaf, because restriction maps are surjective
Any injective sheaf, because $R^i\Gamma(I)=0$ for $i>0$ by definition
The skyscraper sheaf at a point, because it has non-vanishing $H^1$
hard.explain: The constant sheaf $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}$ on $S^1$ has $H^1(S^1,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})\cong\mathbb{Z}$, witnessing the winding-number class. Flabby and injective sheaves are acyclic by construction; skyscraper sheaves at a point have $H^i=0$ for $i>0$ since points are acyclic.

hard.q: For $\mathbb{P}^2_k$, the formula $\dim H^1(\mathbb{P}^2,\mathcal{O}(n))=0$ for all $n$. Using this and the exact sequence $0\to\mathcal{O}(-1)\to\mathcal{O}\to\mathcal{O}_H\to 0$ (restriction to a hyperplane $H\cong\mathbb{P}^1$), compute $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^2,\mathcal{O})$ given $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O})=1$.
hard.explain: The long exact sequence gives $0\to H^0(\mathcal{O}(-1))\to H^0(\mathcal{O})\to H^0(\mathcal{O}_H)\to H^1(\mathcal{O}(-1))\to\cdots$. Since $H^0(\mathcal{O}(-1))=0$ and $H^1(\mathcal{O}(-1))=0$, we get $H^0(\mathbb{P}^2,\mathcal{O})\cong H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O})=1$.

hard.q: Why does the global-sections functor $\Gamma(X,-)$ fail to be right exact on $\mathbf{Ab}(X)$ (abelian sheaves on $X$)?
choices: Because $\Gamma$ does not preserve direct sums
Because a surjection of sheaves $\mathcal{F}\to\mathcal{G}\to 0$ means surjectivity on stalks, not necessarily on global sections — sections may not lift globally
Because $\mathbf{Ab}(X)$ does not have enough injectives
hard.explain: A morphism of sheaves is surjective iff it is surjective on stalks. Global sections need not be surjective: the exponential $\mathcal{O}\to\mathcal{O}^\times$ is locally surjective (every holomorphic function has local logarithms), but $z\in H^0(\mathbb{C}^\times,\mathcal{O}^\times)$ has no global logarithm, so $H^0(\mathcal{O})\to H^0(\mathcal{O}^\times)$ is not surjective.

questions.q: The Picard group of a ringed space $(X,\mathcal{O}_X)$ is canonically isomorphic to which Čech cohomology group?
questions.explain: Line bundles are classified by their transition cocycles in $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ modulo coboundaries: $\mathrm{Pic}(X)\cong\check{H}^1(X,\mathcal{O}_X^\times)$.

questions.q: On $\mathbb{P}^1$ with $\omega_{\mathbb{P}^1}=\mathcal{O}(-2)$, Serre duality gives $\dim H^1(\mathcal{O}(-7))=\dim H^0(\mathcal{O}(7-2))$. Compute this common dimension.
questions.explain: $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(5))=5+1=6$, and $\max(-(-7)-1,0)=6$, so the duality check holds.

questions.q: Use Riemann–Roch on a smooth projective curve of genus $g=4$ together with Serre duality to compute $\dim H^0(C,L)-\dim H^0(C,\omega_C\otimes L^{-1})$ for a line bundle $L$ of degree $d=10$.
questions.explain: Riemann–Roch: $h^0(L)-h^1(L)=d-g+1=10-4+1=7$, and Serre duality identifies $h^1(L)=h^0(\omega_C\otimes L^{-1})$.

hard.q: On an elliptic curve $E$ (genus $1$, $\omega_E\cong\mathcal{O}_E$), Serre duality gives $H^1(E,L)\cong H^0(E,L^{-1})^\vee$. For a degree-$3$ line bundle $L$, which statement is correct?
choices: $\dim H^1(E,L)=0$ because $\deg L>0$ implies $H^1=0$ by Kodaira vanishing
$\dim H^1(E,L)=\dim H^0(E,L^{-1})=0$ since $L^{-1}$ has degree $-3<0$ and $H^0=0$
hard.explain: Serre duality: $H^1(E,L)\cong H^0(E,\omega_E\otimes L^{-1})^\vee = H^0(E,L^{-1})^\vee$. Since $L^{-1}$ has degree $-3<0$, $H^0(E,L^{-1})=0$, so $\dim H^1(E,L)=0$. Then Riemann–Roch gives $\dim H^0(E,L)=\deg L -g+1=3$.

hard.q: For the projective line $\mathbb{P}^1$ and the line bundle $\mathcal{O}(n)$, Serre duality says $h^1(\mathcal{O}(n))=h^0(\mathcal{O}(-n-2))$. Compute $h^1(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(-4))$ using this duality.

The Picard group $\mathrm{Pic}(\mathbb{P}^1_k)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ is generated by $\mathcal{O}(1)$. The exponential sequence on a smooth projective curve $C$ over $\mathbb{C}$ gives an exact sequence involving $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)$. Which statement correctly identifies $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)$?
choices: $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)\cong H^1(C,\mathcal{O}_C)/H^1(C,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})$, which is a complex torus of dimension $g$ (the Jacobian)
hard.explain: From the exponential sequence, the degree-zero line bundles form $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)=\ker(c_1)$. The long exact sequence gives $H^1(C,\mathcal{O})\to H^1(C,\mathcal{O}^\times)\xrightarrow{c_1} H^2(C,\mathbb{Z})$, and $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)\cong H^1(C,\mathcal{O})/\mathrm{image}(H^1(C,\underline{\mathbb{Z}}))$, a $g$-dimensional complex torus — the Jacobian of $C$.
